agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request proposes adding a 'confirmed' status to the Quotes bounded context. The term 'confirmed' is already established in the Orders bounded context with a precise, different meaning. This creates a direct collision in the ubiquitous language — the same word would encode two fundamentally different business states across contexts.","bounded_context":"Quotes (proposed change) — with cross-cutting impact on Orders (existing definition)","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed Order","definition":"An order that has been paid and is queued for fulfillment. It is immutable — no further changes are permitted."},{"term":"Confirmed Quote (PROPOSED — PROBLEMATIC)","definition":"A quote where a customer has verbally agreed to terms but payment has NOT occurred. This is mutable (payment could still fail, customer could withdraw)."},{"term":"Quote","definition":"A priced offer presented to a customer, subject to acceptance and eventual conversion to an order."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A committed transaction that progresses through payment, fulfillment, and delivery."},{"term":"Immutable","definition":"Once a domain object reaches this state, its core business data (line items, pricing, totals) cannot be modified."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"'Confirmed' in the Orders context means paid and immutable. This is an established invariant.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A confirmed order being immutable is a data integrity constraint — downstream fulfillment, invoicing, and shipping all depend on the order not changing after confirmation. Violating this corrupts the pipeline.","testable_assertion":"Given a confirmed order, any attempt to modify line items, pricing, or totals MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A single domain term must have exactly one meaning within the shared enterprise language. 'Confirmed' cannot simultaneously mean 'paid and immutable' and 'verbally agreed, unpaid, mutable'.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If the same term encodes contradictory states, developers, support staff, and integrations will inevitably conflate them, leading to bugs where unpaid quotes are treated as paid orders or vice versa. This is a linguistic integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"No two bounded contexts may define the same status term with contradictory semantics for payment state or mutability."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A verbal agreement from a customer does not constitute payment and does not guarantee conversion to an order.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a financial and legal reality. Verbal agreement is not payment. Treating it as equivalent would misrepresent revenue and could trigger premature fulfillment.","testable_assertion":"A quote with verbal customer agreement MUST NOT be treated as paid, MUST NOT trigger fulfillment, and MUST NOT appear in revenue reporting."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A support agent sees a 'confirmed' quote and a 'confirmed' order in the same customer record and assumes both are paid.","expected_behavior":"This MUST NOT be possible. The naming collision makes this error inevitable with the current proposal."},{"scenario":"An integration or reporting system queries across contexts for all 'confirmed' items and aggregates them as revenue.","expected_behavior":"Unpaid quotes would be incorrectly counted as revenue. The term collision makes this a systemic risk."},{"scenario":"A customer verbally agrees to a quote, the quote is marked 'confirmed', but the customer never pays.","expected_behavior":"The quote should be revocable/expirable. But the term 'confirmed' implies immutability per the established definition, creating confusion about whether the quote can still be withdrawn."},{"scenario":"A developer builds a feature filtering by status='confirmed' across both quotes and orders.","expected_behavior":"The developer would conflate two fundamentally different business states, introducing a defect that may not surface until financial reconciliation."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH RISK. Term collision could lead to premature revenue recognition if 'confirmed' quotes are conflated with 'confirmed' (paid) orders in reporting or accounting systems.","operational":"HIGH RISK. Support, sales, and fulfillment teams will encounter ambiguity. 'Is this confirmed?' becomes an unanswerable question without knowing which context you're in. Training burden increases, error rate increases.","user":"MEDIUM RISK. Internal users (sales agents, support staff) will be confused by the overloaded term. Customer-facing communications using 'confirmed' become ambiguous — does the customer's confirmation email mean they've paid or just agreed?","risk":"CRITICAL. This is a domain language corruption issue. The damage is not immediate but systemic — it degrades the shared understanding that the entire system is built on. Bugs from this class of error are notoriously hard to diagnose because everyone 'understands' the word differently.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales Agents","impact":"Confusion between quote confirmation (verbal) and order confirmation (paid). May prematurely communicate fulfillment timelines to customers."},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"Risk of revenue misstatement if 'confirmed' quotes leak into paid-order reporting."},{"group":"Fulfillment / Warehouse","impact":"Risk of premature picking/packing if a 'confirmed' quote is mistaken for a 'confirmed' order."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Ongoing maintenance burden from disambiguating the same term across contexts. Every query, report, and integration becomes a potential defect site."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Unable to clearly communicate order vs quote status to customers when both use the same word."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction: 'confirmed' is defined as paid+immutable in one context and unpaid+mutable in the proposed context. This is not an ambiguity — it is a collision. Confidence is low because the feature request as written CANNOT be accepted without violating an established invariant of the domain language. The business intent (tracking verbal agreement) is valid, but the proposed implementation term is wrong.","high_confidence_areas":["The term collision is real and dangerous","The business need for tracking verbal agreement on quotes is legitimate","The existing 'confirmed order' invariant (paid + immutable) is well-established"],"low_confidence_areas":["What the organization's preferred alternative term should be — this requires stakeholder input"],"assumptions":["The existing 'confirmed order' definition is authoritative and will not be changed","Quotes and orders share reporting infrastructure where term collision would surface","The organization has customer-facing communications that use the word 'confirmed'"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The term 'confirmed' is already defined in the Orders context as 'paid and immutable.' The proposed Quote 'confirmed' status means 'verbally agreed, unpaid, mutable.' These are mutually exclusive definitions of the same term. This violates the ubiquitous language principle and will cause systemic confusion across sales, finance, fulfillment, and engineering.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Agents","Finance","Fulfillment","Engineering","Customer Support"],"options":["A) Reject the term 'confirmed' for quotes entirely. Use a distinct term such as 'accepted', 'agreed', 'customer-approved', or 'verbally-committed' that accurately encodes the business state without colliding with the Orders context.","B) Rename the existing order status from 'confirmed' to 'paid' or 'committed' and allow quotes to use 'confirmed.' This is high-disruption and not recommended.","C) Accept the collision and rely on context prefixing (e.g., 'quote-confirmed' vs 'order-confirmed'). This is fragile and not recommended."],"recommendation":"Option A. The quote status should use a term like 'accepted' or 'customer-approved.' This preserves the established invariant, accurately describes the business state (customer agreed but hasn't paid), and avoids all downstream confusion. The business need is legitimate — only the naming is wrong."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Ubiquitous language collision: 'confirmed' would mean 'paid + immutable' for orders and 'unpaid + mutable' for quotes simultaneously.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: A single domain term must have exactly one meaning. Two contradictory definitions of 'confirmed' across contexts will cause systemic misunderstanding and defects."},{"violation":"Semantic drift on immutability: 'confirmed' implies immutability per established usage, but a verbally-agreed quote must remain mutable (customer can withdraw, terms can change before payment).","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: 'Confirmed' means paid and immutable. Applying it to an unpaid, mutable entity contradicts this invariant."}]}
